name: Crisp Chat API
what_it_does: Crisp's REST API and real-time messaging (RTM) WebSocket API for managing live chat conversations, contacts, campaigns, and automated messaging across websites and mobile apps. Offers both REST for CRUD operations and a WebSocket RTM layer for real-time event streaming.
use_cases: Building AI chatbot integrations that respond to customer chat messages automatically
Querying and updating conversation status and metadata from support automation agents
Syncing contact data between Crisp and CRM systems programmatically
Triggering outbound chat campaigns or proactive messages based on user behavior
Routing conversations to the right team based on agent-evaluated conversation content
not_for: Teams needing enterprise ticketing with complex SLA management (use Zendesk or Freshdesk)
Email-only support workflows where live chat is irrelevant
Large call centers requiring voice or omnichannel queue management
best_when: An agent needs to participate in or automate live chat conversations on a website, particularly for small-to-mid sized businesses seeking an affordable Intercom alternative.
avoid_when: You need advanced enterprise ITSM features, complex SLA management, or multi-channel support beyond chat and email.

auth.notes: Uses HTTP Basic Auth with an Identifier (API key ID) as the username and a Key (secret) as the password. These are two separate values generated per operator or website level. No OAuth, no token scoping. Compromised key pair provides full account access.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free: unlimited seats, 2 agents active simultaneously, basic chat features
pricing.notes: Generous free tier by live chat standards. Pricing is per website, not per seat, which is unusual and beneficial for larger teams. Pro adds unlimited active agents and automations. Unlimited adds CRM, campaigns, and analytics.

performance.rate_limits: 600 requests/hour per API key pair; RTM WebSocket is event-driven and not rate-limited in the same way

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Error responses include reason and error fields but coverage is inconsistent across endpoints. Some undocumented 4xx errors surface in production use. Error format is not fully standardized across all API endpoints.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: No idempotency keys or deduplication support. Duplicate API calls create duplicate messages. Agents must track state externally to avoid sending messages more than once.
known_agent_gotchas: Real-time events require RTM WebSocket connection, not REST polling — REST alone will miss live messages
API key pair is two separate values (identifier + key); many agents fail to split them correctly for Basic Auth
Rate limit of 600 requests/hour is low for active chat automation — use webhooks or RTM instead of polling
Message sending requires a session_id which must be fetched first; no shortcut to send without a prior lookup
Webhook event types are not fully documented; some events are only discoverable by inspection of live traffic
Website-scoped vs operator-scoped API keys have different conversation visibility — choose correctly for the use case

security_score_components.security_notes: Data hosted in EU, satisfying GDPR requirements. API keys have no fine-grained scopes — a compromised pair provides full account access. Key pairs should be stored securely. No OAuth or token rotation tooling.

score_rationale: Crisp Chat API is accessible and affordable, but agent readiness is limited by a low hourly rate limit, absence of idempotency, inconsistent error documentation, and no API key scoping. The RTM WebSocket requirement for real-time event handling adds architectural complexity. The generous free tier and per-website pricing model make it attractive for small deployments. Best suited for agents handling non-real-time conversation management tasks rather than active chat automation.
